chunks.title: Compiler phases
chunks.text: A compiler translates a source program into a target program (usually machine code or bytecode) in stages. The front end handles lexical analysis, parsing, and semantic analysis — producing an annotated abstract syntax tree (AST) or intermediate representation (IR). The middle end optimizes the IR. The back end generates target code and does machine-specific optimizations. Modern compilers (LLVM, GCC) separate these phases so multiple front ends can share back ends.

chunks.title: Lexical analysis
chunks.text: The lexer (scanner) groups characters into tokens — identifiers, keywords, numbers, operators, punctuation. It strips whitespace and comments. Tokens are usually defined by regular expressions, and the lexer is implemented as a deterministic finite automaton (DFA) generated from those regexes. Tools like flex automate this. The lexer outputs a stream of (token type, lexeme) pairs for the parser to consume.

chunks.title: Parsing
chunks.text: The parser checks that a token stream matches the grammar of the language and builds a parse tree or abstract syntax tree. Top-down parsers (recursive descent, LL(k)) try to derive the input from the start symbol. Bottom-up parsers (LR(k)) build the tree from the leaves up using shift-reduce on a stack. yacc/bison generate LR parsers from a grammar specification. Context-free grammars describe most programming-language syntax.

chunks.title: Semantic analysis
chunks.text: Semantic analysis checks rules that grammar can't express — type correctness, variable scope, declared-before-used, function arity. It walks the AST building a symbol table mapping names to their declarations. Type checking compares each expression's type against what the operator or function expects. Errors at this stage are caught before code generation, sparing the user from seeing them as runtime crashes.

chunks.title: Intermediate representation
chunks.text: The IR is a language-and-machine-independent form sitting between the source and target. Three-address code, static single assignment (SSA), and control flow graphs (CFG) are common IRs. SSA gives every variable exactly one assignment, which makes data-flow analyses simpler. LLVM IR is a widely-used SSA-based representation that lets compilers for many languages target many architectures by going through a shared optimizer.

chunks.title: Code optimization
chunks.text: Optimization improves the IR without changing its meaning. Constant folding evaluates constant expressions at compile time. Dead code elimination removes code whose result is never used. Common subexpression elimination reuses already-computed values. Loop-invariant code motion hoists computations out of loops. Inlining replaces a function call with its body. Optimizations are usually grouped into passes; the order matters because one pass can enable another.

chunks.title: Code generation and register allocation
chunks.text: The back end emits target machine code from the optimized IR. Instruction selection maps IR operations to target instructions. Register allocation assigns variables to a finite set of CPU registers, spilling to memory when there aren't enough — typically formulated as graph colouring on the interference graph. Linear-scan allocation is faster and used in JITs. Peephole optimization makes a final pass to replace short sequences with shorter ones.

chunks.title: Just-in-time compilation
chunks.text: JIT compilers compile code at runtime instead of ahead of time. The JVM and V8 (JavaScript) start by interpreting bytecode and identify hot methods through profiling, then compile them to optimized machine code. Tiered compilation uses multiple JIT levels — a fast baseline JIT first, then heavier optimization for very hot code. Deoptimization unwinds back to the interpreter if a speculative assumption (e.g. about a type) turns out to be wrong.

chunks.title: Regular expressions and finite automata
chunks.text: Regular languages are exactly what regular expressions describe and what DFAs/NFAs recognize. The lexer generator converts each token's regex to an NFA, combines the NFAs, then converts to a DFA (subset construction) for fast execution. Hopcroft's algorithm minimizes the DFA. Most lexers run in O(n) over the input — one DFA state transition per character.

chunks.title: Top-down parsing (LL)
chunks.text: LL(k) parsers read input Left-to-right and produce a Leftmost derivation, looking ahead k tokens. Recursive descent is the most common implementation: one function per non-terminal, mutually recursive. LL(1) (one token of lookahead) requires the grammar to have no left recursion and to satisfy FIRST/FOLLOW set constraints. Hand-written recursive descent parsers (Clang's parser) are simple to debug and produce excellent error messages.

chunks.title: Bottom-up parsing (LR)
chunks.text: LR(k) parsers read input Left-to-right and produce a Rightmost derivation in reverse. They use a stack: shift the next token, then reduce when the top of stack matches a grammar rule's right-hand side. LR(1) handles all deterministic context-free grammars. LALR(1) merges equivalent states for smaller tables (yacc/bison default). SLR is simpler but accepts fewer grammars. LR parsers are usually generated, not hand-written.

chunks.title: Operator precedence parsing
chunks.text: Operator precedence parsing handles expression grammars where each operator has a precedence and associativity. Shunting-yard (Dijkstra) is a classic algorithm: use two stacks (operators and output), push values onto output, pop operators to output when precedence dictates. The Pratt parser is an elegant top-down alternative: each token type has a binding power, and the parser decides whether to consume more tokens based on it.

chunks.title: Syntax-directed translation
chunks.text: Syntax-directed translation embeds semantic actions in grammar rules. As the parser builds the AST, actions compute attributes — type information, addresses, constant values — that propagate up (synthesized) or down (inherited). yacc lets you write { ... C code ... } after each rule. Modern compilers typically separate parsing from semantic analysis, but SDT shaped the design.

chunks.title: Symbol table implementation
chunks.text: The symbol table maps names to their declarations: type, scope, storage location. Implementations: chained hash tables (each scope adds a layer; pop on exit), nested maps. Scope lookup walks from innermost to outermost. Forward references (using a name before declaration) need a second pass or fixup list. The symbol table is consulted by semantic analysis and code generation.

chunks.title: Type systems
chunks.text: A type system categorises program values and the operations on them, catching errors before runtime. Static typing checks at compile time; dynamic at runtime. Strong typing forbids implicit conversions that lose information; weak typing allows them. Structural typing matches by shape; nominal matches by declared name. Modern languages mix these: TypeScript is structural-static; Go is nominal-static; Python is dynamic-strong.

chunks.title: Type inference
chunks.text: Type inference deduces types so the programmer doesn't have to annotate them everywhere. Hindley-Milner (used in ML, Haskell, partially in Rust and Swift) unifies type variables across an expression to find the most general type. Local inference (`var x = 1` in Java, `let x = 1` in TypeScript) is simpler. Inference makes code cleaner without sacrificing static checking.

chunks.title: Polymorphism
chunks.text: Polymorphism lets one piece of code work with many types. Parametric polymorphism (generics): `List<T>` works for any T. Ad-hoc polymorphism (overloading, type classes): different implementations selected by type. Subtype polymorphism (OOP): a function taking Animal also takes Dog. Row polymorphism: functions over records with at least certain fields. Each has different implementation costs (boxing, dispatch overhead).

chunks.title: Subtyping
chunks.text: Subtyping says S is a subtype of T if anywhere a T is expected, an S can be used. The Liskov Substitution Principle formalises this for OOP. Variance rules govern type constructors: List<S> being a subtype of List<T> when S subtypes T (covariance), or the reverse (contravariance for function parameters), or neither (invariance for mutable containers). Misunderstanding variance causes most generics confusion.

chunks.title: Static analysis
chunks.text: Static analysis examines source code without running it to find bugs, security issues, or style violations. Tools: ESLint (JS), Pylint, mypy (Python types), SonarQube. Flow analysis tracks possible values through control flow; symbolic execution explores paths abstractly; abstract interpretation approximates program behaviour with simpler domains. Frama-C and Coverity scale to large C codebases.

chunks.title: Data-flow analysis
chunks.text: Data-flow analysis computes facts about a program's variables at every point. Reaching definitions: which assignments to x might still be active here? Live variables: is x's current value used later? Available expressions: has this expression already been computed without re-assignment of its inputs? Solved by iterative fixed-point over the CFG. Enables many optimizations: dead code elimination, register allocation, constant propagation.

chunks.title: Live variable analysis
chunks.text: A variable is live at a program point if its current value might be used in some future computation before being overwritten. Backward analysis: at the exit, no variables are live; for each statement, in = (out − defs) ∪ uses. Drives dead-code elimination (no live variables means the assignment is dead) and register allocation (only live variables need a register).

chunks.title: Reaching definitions
chunks.text: A definition d of variable x reaches a program point p if some path from d to p doesn't kill d (no intervening redefinition of x). Forward dataflow problem: in[p] = ∪ out[predecessors]; out[p] = gen[p] ∪ (in[p] − kill[p]). Used in constant propagation and use-def chains, which power many later analyses.

chunks.title: Alias analysis
chunks.text: Two pointers alias if they may point to the same memory location. Alias analysis ranges from imprecise-but-fast (any two pointers may alias) to precise-but-expensive (interprocedural, context-sensitive). Critical for optimization — without knowing whether *p and *q overlap, the compiler can't reorder loads and stores or vectorize loops. C99's `restrict` is a programmer-supplied hint that two pointers don't alias.

chunks.title: Loop optimization
chunks.text: Loops are hot — optimizing them pays off. Loop-invariant code motion hoists computations that don't change inside the loop. Loop unrolling reduces branch overhead and exposes more instructions to the CPU. Loop fusion merges adjacent loops with the same iteration space. Vectorization processes multiple iterations in parallel using SIMD instructions (SSE, AVX, NEON). Auto-vectorization is hit-or-miss; modern languages add explicit SIMD types.

chunks.title: Strength reduction
chunks.text: Strength reduction replaces an expensive operation with a cheaper one. Multiplication by a constant becomes a series of shifts and adds (x*5 → (x<<2)+x). Division by a power of two becomes a shift. Inside loops, induction variable reduction converts repeated multiplications to repeated additions. Used to be hand-coded; modern compilers do it automatically.

chunks.title: Tail call optimization
chunks.text: If the last action of a function is a call to another function, the current frame is no longer needed. Tail call optimization reuses the current frame for the callee — no stack growth. This turns recursion into iteration in functional languages (Scheme requires it). Most C/C++ compilers do it opportunistically; JavaScript spec includes it but implementations skipped it; Python deliberately doesn't to keep stack traces clear.

chunks.title: Inlining
chunks.text: Inlining replaces a function call with the callee's body, eliminating call overhead and exposing the body to more optimizations in the caller's context. Modern compilers decide based on size (small functions usually inlined), call frequency (hot functions inlined), and whether the callee is visible (cross-module inlining needs LTO). Over-inlining bloats code and hurts I-cache; under-inlining leaves performance on the table.

chunks.title: Profile-guided optimization
chunks.text: PGO uses runtime profile data — which branches are taken, which functions are hot — to guide optimization decisions. Build twice: instrumented build collects profile; production build uses it. Compilers can lay out code so hot paths are together (better I-cache), inline hot calls more aggressively, and unroll loops with high trip counts. Commonly 5-15% performance improvement for compiled languages.

chunks.title: Garbage collection algorithms
chunks.text: Mark-and-sweep traces from roots (stack, globals), marks reachable objects, then sweeps unmarked ones into the free list. Copying collectors split the heap in two, copy live objects from old to new, swap. Generational GC exploits the fact that most objects die young — collect the young generation often, old generation rarely. Reference counting increments/decrements counts on assignment; immediate but can't handle cycles.

chunks.title: Reference counting vs tracing GC
chunks.text: Reference counting (Swift, CPython) frees objects immediately when their count hits zero — predictable, no pause times, but cycle leaks need a backup tracing collector and atomic increments cost in concurrent code. Tracing GC (Java, Go, JavaScript) periodically walks the heap from roots, freeing unreachable objects. Modern tracing GCs are concurrent and incremental, keeping pauses sub-millisecond.

chunks.title: Mark-and-sweep details
chunks.text: The mark phase walks roots and follows pointers, marking each reached object. The sweep phase walks the heap linearly, reclaiming unmarked objects. Issues: heap fragmentation (sweep doesn't compact), poor locality. Mark-and-compact addresses this by moving live objects together after marking. Conservative GC (Boehm) treats any word that looks like a pointer as one — works for C/C++.

chunks.title: Generational GC
chunks.text: Generational GC partitions the heap into generations (young, old). Most allocation happens in young; most objects die young; collecting only young (minor GC) is fast. Surviving objects get promoted to old. Old generation is collected less often (major GC) and may use a different algorithm (mark-and-compact). Java's G1, ZGC, and Shenandoah all use generational ideas with different trade-offs.

chunks.title: Continuation-passing style
chunks.text: CPS transforms a program so that every function takes an extra continuation argument representing 'what to do next'. Instead of returning, functions tail-call the continuation. This makes control flow explicit and tail calls universal, simplifying many compiler analyses. SML/NJ uses CPS-based intermediate code. Many JavaScript transpilers (for async/await) generate CPS-flavoured code under the hood.

chunks.title: Linkers and loaders
chunks.text: The linker combines object files (compiled translation units) into an executable, resolving symbol references between them. Static linking copies all needed code into the output; dynamic linking leaves shared library references to be resolved at load time, saving disk and memory but adding runtime cost. The loader maps the executable into memory, sets up the stack, and jumps to the entry point. ld is the classic Unix linker; lld is LLVM's faster alternative.
